questions.explanation: This question asks for the INCORRECT sentence. "Omar don't like coffee" is wrong because Omar = he, and he/she/it needs "doesn't": Omar doesn't like coffee ✓. The other three sentences correctly use "don't" with they, we and "doesn't" with she.

questions.prompt: Rania gets up at six o'clock. Choose the question that asks about this time.

options.text: What time does Rania get up?
What time do Rania get up?
What time is Rania get up?

questions.explanation: A simple present question needs: WH-word + does (for he/she/it) + subject + BASE verb → What time does Rania get up? ✓ The common traps are all here: "do" with a singular subject, "is" mixed with an ordinary verb, and forgetting the auxiliary completely ("What time Rania gets up?").

questions.prompt: Anis goes to football training three Saturdays out of every four. Which sentence describes his routine?

options.text: He always goes to football training on Saturdays.
He usually goes to football training on Saturdays.
He sometimes goes to football training on Saturdays.
He never goes to football training on Saturdays.

questions.explanation: Three Saturdays out of four is most of the time, but not every time — that is exactly "usually" ✓. The common trap is "always", which means 100% with no exception; "sometimes" would fit a much lower frequency, and "never" means 0%.

questions.prompt: "It's ten to nine." Which clock time is it?
questions.explanation: "Ten to nine" means 10 minutes BEFORE nine o'clock: 9:00 minus 10 minutes = 8:50 ✓. The common trap is 9:10, which is "ten past nine" — "past" counts after the hour, "to" counts before the next hour. Reading the words in order as "10:09" is also wrong.

questions.prompt: In which word is the vowel sound /ɑː/, as in "father"?
questions.explanation: "Park" has the long sound /ɑː/, exactly like "father" ✓. The trap is that "mother" and "brother" LOOK like "father" on paper, but they are pronounced with the short sound /ʌ/, like "cup" and "sun" — spelling and sound do not always match in English.

questions.prompt: Complete: "My cousins ___ got a small dog, and they enjoy ___ with it in the garden."
questions.explanation: Two rules work together here: "cousins" is plural (= they), so it takes "have got", and after "enjoy" the activity takes -ing → have … playing ✓. The common trap is getting one rule right and the other wrong: "has" with a plural subject, or "enjoy play" without -ing.
